entryGuidance.firstAction: Change the target position, then rerun the search with Interval width over time open.
entryGuidance.watchFor: Watch low, mid, and high jump inward together as the remaining interval width drops by about half after each midpoint check.

featuredSetups.label: Far-right target
featuredSetups.description: Open a large ordered list where the target sits near the far edge.

setup.note: Use this to see how quickly the interval shrinks even when the target is far from the first midpoint.

featuredSetups.label: Middle target
featuredSetups.description: Open a clean case where the midpoint hits the target quickly.

setup.note: Use this when you want a simple first look at low, mid, and high.

pageIntro.whyItMatters: It turns the binary-search invariant—the target always stays inside low..high—into a visible shrinking interval, so logarithmic efficiency feels earned instead of memorized.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: Because the list is ordered, each midpoint comparison keeps the half that can still contain the target and discards the other half.
pageIntro.definition: Binary search repeatedly checks the middle of a sorted interval, keeps the half that could still contain the target, and preserves the low-mid-high invariant as the interval shrinks.

v2.intuition: Think of binary search as a promise you keep: if the target exists, it stays inside the highlighted low-to-high interval while each midpoint check cuts that promise roughly in half.
v2.whyItMatters: This is the clean bridge from sorting into ordered search: sorted order lets one comparison delete half the possibilities while preserving the answer-still-inside invariant.

equationSnapshot.note: The midpoint tells you which value to test next; the interval width shows how the low-to-high invariant shrinks after each comparison.

guidedSteps.title: See the live interval
guidedSteps.goal: Read low, mid, high, and the active interval as the invariant: the target must still be inside this highlighted window.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start from Middle target, keep Interval width over time open, and name the promise before each check: the target is still somewhere between low and high.
guidedSteps.notice: Binary search is not scanning a list one slot at a time. It is protecting a shrinking interval that still contains every possible target position.
guidedSteps.explain: low and high bracket the only positions still possible; mid is the single test that decides which half can be safely discarded.

guidedSteps.title: Push the target to an edge
guidedSteps.goal: See that halving still works even when the target is far from the first midpoint.
guidedSteps.doThis: Switch to Far-right target and keep Interval width over time open while you watch low jump past the midpoint.
guidedSteps.notice: A far target can still be found through only a few midpoint checks because the surviving interval keeps losing half its width.
guidedSteps.explain: The advantage does not come from guessing the exact target early. It comes from shrinking the remaining search space aggressively each time.

guidedSteps.title: Use order to discard half
guidedSteps.goal: Tie the interval invariant directly to sorted order: one comparison is enough to choose the surviving half.
guidedSteps.doThis: Keep the low-mid-high history visible and say which half survives after each midpoint comparison.
guidedSteps.notice: The midpoint only tells you which half to keep because every value on one side of it is already ordered relative to the target.
guidedSteps.explain: Without order, a midpoint comparison cannot justify deleting half the interval while still promising that the target remains inside low..high.

guidedSteps.title: Check the halving story
guidedSteps.goal: Pressure-test whether you can explain binary search without falling back on vague efficiency slogans.
guidedSteps.doThis: Use the quick checks, then make one large-list case where the target sits near an edge but the search still finishes quickly.
guidedSteps.notice: The clearest answer keeps pointing back to the same live fact: the active interval keeps shrinking by halves.
guidedSteps.explain: If you can say why ordered data matters and why the interval width drops so quickly, you are ready for the full concept test and the graph-search follow-up.

learned: Binary search works by maintaining one invariant: if the target exists, it is still inside the active low-to-high interval.
Each midpoint comparison can delete half of the remaining positions because the list is ordered.
A far-away target can still be found quickly once the interval keeps halving.
wrapUp.misconception: Binary search is not just a slightly faster scan. Its real advantage is using ordered data to discard half the remaining positions without breaking the answer-still-inside invariant.

nextConcepts.title: Graph Representation and Adjacency Intuition
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Move from ordered intervals into graph neighborhoods and visible frontiers.

nextConcepts.title: Sorting and Algorithmic Trade-offs
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Return to the same list language and compare ordered search with visible sorting work.

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Stay on the list bench and build your own middle-target and edge-target cases until you can predict which half will survive before each midpoint check.

paragraphs: Binary search only makes sense on ordered data. It keeps a simple invariant: if the target exists, it is still somewhere between low and high.
Each midpoint comparison uses the sorted order to decide which half cannot contain the target, then moves low or high inward while keeping that promise true.
This bench keeps low, mid, high, and the shrinking interval visible together so the speed comes from the geometry of the search space, not from memorizing a procedure.

keyIdeas: Binary search works because sorted order lets one midpoint comparison discard a whole half.
The invariant is: the target, if it exists, stays inside the current low-to-high interval.
Low, mid, and high define the live search space at every step.

commonMisconception.myth: Binary search is just a faster way of checking one item at a time.
correction: The point is not a slightly better scan. The point is keeping the answer inside the active interval while halving the remaining possibilities after each midpoint check.
That only works because the list is already ordered.

workedExamples.title: Live worked example
workedExamples.intro: Read the current interval directly from the live search scene.

items.title: Read the current search interval
items.prompt: What does the current binary-search step tell you right now?

steps.label: 1. Read the live interval
steps.template: The active interval runs from index {{lowIndexValue}} to {{highIndexValue}}, so {{intervalWidthValue}} positions are still possible.

steps.label: 2. Read the midpoint check
steps.template: The current midpoint is index {{midIndexValue}}, which holds {{midValueValue}} while the target is {{targetValueValue}}.

steps.label: 3. Compare the search cost
steps.template: So far binary search has used {{comparisonsValue}} midpoint checks, while a one-by-one scan would need {{linearNeedValue}} checks to guarantee the same target.

applyAction.label: Apply the far-right target setup
applyAction.observationHint: Watch how a far target can still be found through a very small number of midpoint checks.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a case where the target is near the far edge of a large list but binary search still reaches it in only a few midpoint checks.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether the target should sit near the middle or near an edge before you change the controls.
miniChallenge.answer: Put the target near an edge in a large list, then let halving do the work.
miniChallenge.explanation: Even a far-away target can be found quickly once each midpoint check keeps the correct half and preserves the low-to-high invariant.

equations.label: Midpoint rule
equations.meaning: Chooses the next index to inspect from the current live interval.

equations.label: Interval width
equations.meaning: Counts how many positions are still alive after each halving step.

variableLinks.label: Array size
variableLinks.description: Sets how many ordered values the search has to work through.

variableLinks.label: Target position
variableLinks.description: Moves the target through the ordered list while keeping the array itself sorted.

variableLinks.label: Linear contrast
variableLinks.description: Shows how a one-by-one scan would progress through the same ordered list.

presets.label: Middle target
presets.description: A clean first case where the midpoint is already close to the target.

presets.label: Far-right target
presets.description: A larger case where the target sits near the far edge of the list.

presets.label: Left-edge target
presets.description: A case where the first midpoint is far from the target, but halving still converges quickly.

overlays.label: Active interval
overlays.shortDescription: Highlight the part of the ordered list that is still alive.
whatToNotice: Each midpoint comparison can delete half the list at once.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the efficiency of binary search into something visible.

overlays.label: Low-mid-high markers
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current low, mid, and high markers on the ordered list.
whatToNotice: Midpoint checks matter because low and high are already bracketing the live search space.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps binary search anchored to the current interval instead of to jargon.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the ordered list and one history graph visible together.

items.text: The important move is not a faster scan. It is keeping the target inside the active interval while the midpoint deletes half of that interval at once.
The midpoint only tells you which half to keep because the list is ordered.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict the next surviving half before you advance the scene.

items.prompt: If the target value is larger than the current midpoint value, which half should survive?

choices.label: The right half survives, so low moves up past mid.
The left half survives, so high moves down below mid.

items.explanation: Because the list is ordered, every value left of mid is too small if the midpoint is already too small.
items.observationHint: Open the far-right target setup and watch low jump past mid after each failed check.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live search picture, not from memorized vocabulary.

questions.prompt: Why does binary search need ordered data to preserve its interval invariant?

choices.label: Because only then can one midpoint comparison discard half the interval while keeping the target inside the surviving half.
Because ordered data makes every midpoint equal to the target.
Because ordering changes the size of the array.

questions.explanation: Without order, the midpoint gives you no honest reason to remove half the remaining positions while preserving the target-still-inside promise.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: A midpoint is only sometimes the target; ordering is what makes safe discards possible.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Ordering changes arrangement, not the number of items.

questions.prompt: What is the clearest reason binary search feels fast on the bench?

choices.label: It secretly checks every item at once.
The active interval keeps shrinking by halves.
It works even better on unsorted data.

questions.explanation: The speed comes from reducing the search space aggressively after each midpoint check.

selectedWrongExplanations.a: Binary search checks one midpoint at a time, not every item at once.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Without ordering, the half-discard invariant breaks.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Make halving the search space do real work.

items.title: Find a far-right target fast
items.prompt: Build a large ordered list where the target sits near the far-right edge, but binary search still finds it in five checks or fewer.
items.successMessage: You built the right kind of binary-search scene: even a far-right target in a large list still collapsed quickly because the interval kept halving.

paragraphs: The simulation shows an ordered array with low, mid, and high markers. The active interval stays highlighted, and an optional ghost lane shows how far a one-by-one scan would have progressed by the same number of checks.
One graph tracks the interval width, another tracks the low-mid-high positions, and a third compares binary-search checks with linear-search checks.

seo.title: Binary Search / Halving the Search Space
seo.description: Watch low, mid, and high shrink the live interval on an ordered list so binary search feels visual instead of procedural.
